1: Polemos kai machai en hymin (wars and conflicts among you) — polemos (war, armed conflict) and mache (fight, battle) applied to community disputes. The origin James identifies — ek tōn hēdonōn hymōn tōn strateuomenōn en tois melesin hymōn (from your pleasures that are warring in your members) — uses military imagery for inner conflict: the pleasures themselves wage war within the body. The Stoic and Platonic traditions analyzed inner conflict as the war of irrational desires against reason; James gives this a more specifically social application — inner disorder produces communal conflict.
2: Epithymeite kai ouk echete (you desire and do not have) — the sequence of unfulfilled desire leading to violence echoes Micah 2:2: "They covet fields and seize them; houses, and take them away." The failure to ask God (James 4:2b: "you do not have because you do not ask") diagnoses the community's conflict as a failure of prayer: they seek satisfaction through social competition rather than petitionary trust. The Jewish prayer tradition assumed that unmet need was the appropriate occasion for petition; James's community has bypassed prayer in favor of conflict.
3: Aitēte kai ou lambanete, dioti kakōs aiteisthe (you ask and do not receive because you ask badly) — the wrong motive in prayer (hina en tais hēdonais hymōn dapanēsēte, to spend on your own pleasures) disqualifies the request. The tradition of prayer heard and unanswered runs through the psalms of lament (Psalms 22:2: "I cry but you do not answer") and the wisdom tradition; James locates the problem not in divine unwillingness but in the petitioner's orientation. Prayer that seeks self-gratification rather than God's will is not genuine petition.
4: Moichalides (adulteresses) — the prophetic address to Israel as YHWH's unfaithful spouse (Hosea 2:2-13; Jeremiah 3:1-10; Ezekiel 16:15-58) frames the community's "friendship with the world" as spiritual adultery. The marriage-covenant between YHWH and Israel provided the prophets with their most powerful image for Israel's unfaithfulness; James applies it to the community's desire for the world's approval and goods. Echthra tou theou (enmity toward God) — echthros tou theou (enemy of God) was the most extreme statement of covenantal breach.
5: The difficult verse — "Or do you think the Scripture speaks without purpose? 'The Spirit he made to dwell in us longs jealously'" — cites a text not identifiable in the OT canon. Possible sources include Exodus 20:5 (the jealous God who will not share his people with other gods), Numbers 11:29, or an apocryphal text. The divine jealousy (epipothei... phthonon) for the Spirit he has placed in his people recalls the prophetic tradition of YHWH's jealous love for Israel (Isaiah 63:10-11; Ezekiel 36:26-27). The Spirit given at the new creation longs for undivided devotion.
6: The explicit Scriptural citation — ho theos hyperēphanois antitassetai, tapeinois de didōsin charin (God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble) — is from Proverbs 3:34 LXX (which differs from the MT). The same verse is cited in 1 Peter 5:5. The divine opposition to pride (antitassomai, to arrange against, to set in battle array against) and the divine grace to the humble (tapeinois) make God himself the adversary of the proud. The community that pursues worldly honor (James 4:4) finds itself in battle against the God who opposes pride.
7: Hypotageite oun tō theō (submit therefore to God) — the military submission vocabulary from the honor-and-authority tradition; the hypo-tassō (arrange under) of voluntary subordination to the divine authority. Antistēte de tō diabolō kai pheuxetai aph' hymōn (resist the devil and he will flee from you) — the two-movements instruction: submission toward God and resistance toward the devil. The tradition of the devil's flight at resistance is rooted in the understanding that the devil operates as an accuser and tempter but lacks ultimate power over those who are firmly aligned with God.
8: Eggisate tō theō kai eggiei hymin (draw near to God and he will draw near to you) — the mutual-drawing-near language echoes the prophetic call to return (Zechariah 1:3: "Return to me and I will return to you"; Malachi 3:7). The spatial image of approaching the deity was central to priestly and temple theology: the worshipper drew near to the divine presence at the altar. James applies it to the posture of the heart. Katharisate cheiras... hagnisate kardias (cleanse hands... purify hearts) — the ritual purity vocabulary (hands = external conduct; heart = internal motivation) reflects the prophetic synthesis of Isaiah 1:16-17 and Psalms 24:4.
9: Talaipōrēsate kai penthēsate kai klausate (be wretched and mourn and weep) — the call to lamentation. The reversal of laughter to mourning (ho gelōs hymōn eis penthos metastraphētō kai hē chara eis katēpheian) inverts the joy that the world offers into the mourning that authentic repentance produces. This is the language of prophetic lament (Joel 1:13-14; James 2:12-13) — the community is called to the kind of communal mourning that accompanies genuine return to God. The joy of repentance that follows the mourning is the beatitude pattern (Matthew 5:4: "blessed are those who mourn, for they will be comforted").
10: Tapeinōthēte enōpion Kyriou kai hypsōsei hymas (humble yourselves before the Lord and he will exalt you) — the reversal pattern that runs through the wisdom and prophetic traditions: the humble are exalted, the proud are brought low (1 Samuel 2:7-8; Psalms 147:6; Proverbs 3:34; Luke 1:52-53; Matthew 23:12). The passive hypsōsei (he will exalt) is a divine passive: the exaltation comes from God, not from self-promotion. This is the opposite of the worldly honor-seeking of James 4:4 (friendship with the world for the sake of standing).
11: Mē katalaleite allēlōn (do not slander one another) — the prohibition of katalaleō (to speak down against, slander) targets the speech-act that claims judicial authority over another member of the community. Ho katalālōn adelphou ē krinōn ton adelphon autou katalālei nomou kai krinei nomon (the one who slanders a brother or judges his brother slanders the law and judges the law) — to usurp the role of judge over a fellow member is to usurp the law's role, and thereby to place oneself above the law rather than under it.
12: Heis estin ho nomothetēs kai kritēs (there is one Lawgiver and Judge) — the divine monopoly on legislation and judgment grounds the prohibition of human judgment over one another. The Jewish tradition of the divine as the sole Lawgiver (Exodus 20:1-17; Deuteronomy 5:6-21) and the sole Judge (Genesis 18:25: "Shall not the Judge of all the earth do right?") removes legislative and judicial authority from human community members. The question sy de tis ei ho krinōn ton plēsion (but who are you who judges your neighbor?) — echoes Ezekiel 22:2 and the prophetic challenge to self-appointed judges.
13: Age nyn hoi legontes (come now, you who say) — the diatribe's direct challenge to a specific group within the community. The merchants who plan travel and commerce (emporeusomaethia kai kerdasomen — we will trade and make a profit) are not condemned for trading but for the presumption of autonomous planning that ignores divine sovereignty over time and life. The first-century Jewish diaspora included significant merchant communities whose commercial travel through the Mediterranean world was both economically necessary and culturally distinctive.
14: Atmis gar este hē pros oligon phainomenē, epeita kai aphanizomenē (for you are a mist that appears for a little while and then disappears) — the brevity-of-life image. Atmis (mist, vapor, steam) echoes Ecclesiastes' hebel (vapor, breath, vanity — the word used 38 times in Ecclesiastes for the fleeting character of human life and endeavor). The morning mist that burns off when the sun rises is a Palestinan agricultural reality; James uses it as Qohelet uses hebel — to locate human planning within the context of human transience.
15: Ean ho Kyrios thelēsē kai zēsomen kai poiēsomen touto ē ekeino (if the Lord wills, we will live and do this or that) — the formula of divine-will acknowledgment. The equivalent formula in the Jewish tradition was the Aramaic im yirtzeh ha-Shem (if God wills) or Greek ean ho theos thelē — a recognized expression of creaturely dependence on divine sovereignty over the future. Paul uses equivalent formulas (1 Corinthians 4:19; James 16:7; Acts 18:21). The formula is not fatalism but the theological recognition that human agency is embedded in divine providence.
16: Nyn de kauchasthe en tais alazoneiais hymōn (but as it is, you boast in your arrogances) — alazoneia (boasting, arrogance, pretension) was the vice of the alazōn (the braggart, the one who claims more than they possess) — a stock figure in comedy and diatribe as the target of satirical critique. Aristotle contrasted alazoneia (excess self-presentation) with eirōneia (self-deprecation) and alētheia (truthful self-presentation) as the vices flanking the virtue of honest self-assessment. The boasting merchants claim more control over the future than they possess.
17: Eidoti oun kalon poiein kai mē poionti hamartia autō estin (to the one who knows the right thing to do and does not do it, it is sin for him) — the closing principle of James 4 is the wisdom tradition's foundational insight: sin is not only active transgression but the failure to act on known obligation. The Greek philosophical tradition (Aristotle's akrasia, weakness of will) and the Jewish halakhic tradition both wrestled with the gap between knowing the good and doing it. James closes the chapter with this principle to encompass all the failures he has described: those who know what God requires and yet choose worldly friendship, pride, judgment of others, and autonomous planning are all instances of this single pattern.
